format_discipline.notes: No frontmatter — first non-empty line is '# Spec-first discipline…' H1. reading.meta.json present and parses with all required fields (word_count, estimated_read_minutes, citations_used, sections). Exactly 6 fixed sections in correct order: Overview / Key Concepts / Worked Example / In Practice / Key Takeaways / References. No sections added, renamed, or dropped.

citation_discipline.notes: All 5 primary resources cited at least once. [1] Fowler cited in Overview, Key Concepts (x4), In Practice, Key Takeaways. [2] Joel on Software cited in Overview, Key Concepts (x4), Worked Example, In Practice, Key Takeaways. [3] Prompting Guide cited in Key Concepts (x2), Key Takeaways. [4] Real Python cited in Key Concepts, Worked Example, Key Takeaways. [5] Anthropic cited in Key Concepts (x2), Key Takeaways. Bibliography in References lists all 5 matching inline markers. No orphan markers; no orphan bibliography entries.

forward_references.notes: Topics 11.8 and 11.9 appear only as named-and-deferred pointers: diagram caption (line 35 'introduced in topics 11.8 and 11.9'), workflow list steps 3–4 labelled '(11.8)' and '(11.9)', and Key Takeaways bullet 'explain every line (11.8) → test edge cases (11.9)'. None of these teach the deferred content. No other forward references to topics beyond 11.7 detected.

beginner_readability.notes: All non-everyday terms defined on first use: 'specification (spec for short)' on line 5; Inputs/Outputs/Constraints/Examples introduced with a table in Key Concepts showing the question each answers and a concrete example. No jargon dumps — each sub-section introduces one idea. No walls of text — longest prose paragraphs are 3–4 sentences. Sequences rendered as numbered or bulleted lists (4-step workflow list, In Practice bullets). Concrete inline examples present throughout Key Concepts (grade-average data used in table and in prompt example). Bold-term / plain-meaning pattern used consistently. No assessment references (no mention of A4, due week, or grade weight).

faithfulness.notes: 10 assertions sampled across all sections and traced to corpus: (1) specification definition → corpus §5.1; (2) four-part table structure → corpus §5.1; (3) spec=what/code=how distinction → corpus §5.1; (4) writing-forces-precision reason → corpus §5.2 Reason 1; (5) spec-you-cannot-write reason → corpus §5.2 Reason 2; (6) verification-standard reason → corpus §5.2 Reason 3; (7) spec-as-prompt reason → corpus §5.2 Reason 4; (8) good-vs-bad spec comparison → corpus §5.4; (9) spec-as-prompt example sentence → corpus §5.5; (10) comments-echo-the-spec In Practice bullet → corpus §8. Zero unsupported assertions found. Reading is a faithful condensed pass over the corpus, dropping the Carol example from the good spec (acceptable condensation) and omitting §6 implementation detail and §9 hands-on exercise (appropriate for learner-facing reading).

verdict: PASS (cycle 1). All 6 checks pass. Format clean — no frontmatter, correct 6-section template, valid meta sidecar. Word count 1285 within 1200–1600; 7 min read within 6–8 min target. All 5 primary resources cited with matching bibliography. Forward references to 11.8 and 11.9 are correctly named-and-deferred only. Beginner readability strong — all terms defined on first use, no jargon dumps, no walls of text, sequences listed, inline examples present throughout. No scope creep — reading stays strictly on spec-first discipline with no Python syntax taught. All 10 sampled faithfulness assertions trace cleanly to corpus. No hard fails. No soft finding escalations. Ready for HITL #3.
